Conflicting feedback is resolved by the primary metric and guardrail thresholds — you optimize for the primary, don't violate guardrails, and use diagnostics for debugging.

[Concrete Numbers]: At Meta, the primary metric for feed ranking is "meaningful social interactions" (MSI). Guardrail metrics: ad revenue (must not drop >2%), user session time (must not drop >5%), content diversity (HHI on content sources must not drop >0.05). A change that increases MSI by 3% but drops ad revenue by 5% is rejected — the guardrail fires. A change that increases MSI by 5% and drops ad revenue by 1% is approved. At Google Search, the primary metric is "user satisfaction" (measured by search abandonment rate). Guardrails: ad revenue (notify if >3% drop), page load speed (p99 <500ms), result freshness (at least 30% new results per page).

[Practical Workflow]: 1) Define the primary metric that captures your core objective (usually an online metric measurable via A/B test), 2) Define guardrail metrics with hard thresholds (modeled after business constraints — revenue, engagement, cost, fairness), 3) For every model change: run A/B test, compare primary metric (must show statistically significant improvement), check guardrail metrics (must NOT show statistically significant degradation), 4) If primary improves but guardrail degrades → reject the change and investigate, 5) If primary improves and guardrails are neutral → approve, 6) If primary is neutral but guardrails improve → consider approving (risk-free improvement in operational efficiency).

[Interview Angle]: Meta E6/Netflix L5 | Tests product-sense + metric design — a Staff+ ML engineer must define the evaluation framework, not just optimize a given metric.

[Common Mistakes]: Having too many primary metrics (conflicting objectives without resolution — "maximize A, B, and C"). Not setting guardrail thresholds explicitly (debating every result). Choosing guardrails that are too tight (blocks all innovation) or too loose (allows degradation that compounds over time).
sections.codeBlock:
# Multi-metric evaluation framework
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats

class MultiMetricEvaluator:
    """
    Evaluate a model change against primary metric and guardrails.
    
    Primary: metric we're optimizing (need sig improvement)
    Guardrails: metrics that must not degrade beyond threshold
    """
    def __init__(self, primary_metric, guardrails, alpha=0.05):
        """
        primary_metric: {'name': str, 'direction': 'higher'|'lower'}
        guardrails: [{'name': str, 'direction': 'higher'|'lower', 'max_degradation': float}]
        """
        self.primary = primary_metric
        self.guardrails = guardrails
        self.alpha = alpha
    
    def evaluate(self, control_metrics, treatment_metrics):
        """
        control_metrics: dict of metric_name -> array of values
        treatment_metrics: dict of metric_name -> array of values
        
        Returns: {'approved': bool, 'reason': str, 'details': dict}
        """
        # Check primary metric
        prim_control = control_metrics[self.primary['name']]
        prim_treatment = treatment_metrics[self.primary['name']]
        
        stat, pval = stats.ttest_ind(prim_control, prim_treatment)
        
        prim_improved = (
            (np.mean(prim_treatment) > np.mean(prim_control))
            if self.primary['direction'] == 'higher'
            else (np.mean(prim_treatment) < np.mean(prim_control))
        )
        prim_significant = pval < self.alpha
        
        if not (prim_improved and prim_significant):
            return {
                'approved': False,
                'reason': f"Primary metric {self.primary['name']} did not show significant improvement (p={pval:.4f})",
                'details': {}
            }
        
        # Check guardrails
        guardrail_results = {}
        for guardrail in self.guardrails:
            g_control = control_metrics[guardrail['name']]
            g_treatment = treatment_metrics[guardrail['name']]
            
            g_mean_control = np.mean(g_control)
            g_mean_treatment = np.mean(g_treatment)
            degradation = (g_mean_treatment - g_mean_control) / g_mean_control
            
            # Check if degradation exceeds threshold
            degraded = (
                degradation < -guardrail['max_degradation']
                if guardrail['direction'] == 'higher'
                else degradation > guardrail['max_degradation']
            )
            
            guardrail_results[guardrail['name']] = {
                'mean_control': g_mean_control,
                'mean_treatment': g_mean_treatment,
                'degradation_pct': degradation * 100,
                'guardrail_failed': degraded
            }
        
        failed_guardrails = [
            g for g, r in guardrail_results.items()
            if r['guardrail_failed']
        ]
        
        if failed_guardrails:
            return {
                'approved': False,
                'reason': f"Guardrail violation: {', '.join(failed_guardrails)}",
                'details': guardrail_results
            }
        
        return {
            'approved': True,
            'reason': "Primary metric improved, all guardrails passed",
            'details': guardrail_results
        }

sections.title: 30. FAANG Staff+ Bonus: Curriculum Czar Questions
sections.description:
These questions test the full breadth of Staff-level ML knowledge — system-level thinking, cross-domain knowledge, and the ability to make informed tradeoffs without perfect information.

[Interview Angle] Q1 (Google L6): "A team trained a model that achieves 99% accuracy on the test set but fails catastrophically in production. What's your debugging process?" Answer: Start with the overfit-one-batch test to rule out implementation bugs. Then check for data drift (PSI per feature, KS test). If no drift, check for concept drift (label distribution shift — the test set labels were from 2022, production is 2026). If no drift, check for training-serving skew (feature pipeline inconsistency — batch features computed differently in training vs serving). Run shadow deployment to compare offline predictions vs real-time predictions. In 80% of cases, the root cause is training-serving skew or data pipeline issues, not the model itself.

[Interview Angle] Q2 (Meta E6): "How do you design a fair ranking system that satisfies users, content creators, AND advertisers?" Answer: Three-sided marketplace optimization. Define metrics per stakeholder: users (satisfaction, diversity), creators (reach, fairness), advertisers (ROI, delivery). Use constrained optimization: maximize user satisfaction subject to (1) creator reach fairness (Gini coefficient < 0.6), (2) advertiser ROI guarantee (minimum 3:1 ROAS). In practice, this is solved with a weighted combination of predicted metrics and a fairness penalty term. Monitor for "Matthew effect" (rich get richer — popular content gets more engagement, dominating less popular but diverse content).

[Interview Angle] Q3 (Amazon L6): "Your ML team is building 5 models for 5 different use cases. How do you decide whether to build separate models or one multi-task model?" Answer: Build separate models if: tasks have different data distributions, different latency requirements, different update frequencies, or different stakeholders. Build one multi-task model if: tasks share features, tasks have limited data individually, total inference cost dominates (N separate models cost N× more). Practical approach: start with a shared backbone (BERT) + task-specific heads. Monitor for negative transfer (multi-task hurting individual task performance). If negative transfer exceeds 5% per task, split into separate models. At Amazon, most teams use 1 model per use case because deployment flexibility outweighs engineering efficiency.

[Interview Angle] Q4 (Apple Staff): "Design an on-device ML system for a privacy-preserving health monitoring feature. Model must be <5MB, inference <5ms, battery impact <1% per day." Answer: Use federated learning with differential privacy (ε=4, δ=10^-6). Model: MobileNet-V3 (2.5M params, 4.5MB in INT8) for feature extraction + lightweight classifier. On-device training: fine-tune only the classifier head (10K params) on user data. Sync encrypted gradient updates to server when charging + on WiFi. Model update: server-aggregated global model distributed as delta (only changed weights, ~10KB). Privacy: all health data stays on-device — server never sees raw data. Power: ANE-based inference uses 0.5mW vs 100mW on CPU — critical for <1% battery budget.

[Interview Angle] Q5 (OpenAI Staff): "How would you measure whether a model is becoming more capable vs just memorizing the training data?" Answer: The key distinction is generalization — a more capable model solves NEW problems, a memorizing model only recalls seen patterns. Metrics: (1) For novel task generalization, create held-out tasks that require combining skills seen in training in unseen ways (e.g., compositional generalization), (2) For reasoning, measure performance on out-of-distribution reasoning chains (same logic, different content — GSM8K→SVAMP), (3) For memorization, use the "Ekin test" — ask the model to generate training data verbatim then cross-check exact matches against training corpus (deduplicated benchmarks). The best models (GPT-4, o1) show near-zero memorization on deduplicated benchmarks and high generalization on novel compositions.

[Key Takeaway]: Staff-level questions test your ability to make principled tradeoffs, debug systematically, and design evaluation frameworks — not just recall facts. Practice by connecting concepts across domains (e.g., "how does the Chinchilla scaling law affect my recommendation system architecture?").

[Common Mistakes]: Giving a memorized answer without adapting to the question's specifics. Not considering cost and practical constraints. Failing to acknowledge uncertainty — Staff-level answers should include "it depends" with the reasoning for each branch.
